From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

"Presidents of the United States" redirects here. For other uses, see [President of the United States (disambiguation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/President_of_the_United_States_(disambiguation) "President of the United States (disambiguation)").

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/White_House_Washington.JPG/330px-White_House_Washington.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:White_House_Washington.JPG)

The [White House](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/White_House "White House"), official residence of the president of the United States

The [president of the United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") is the [head of state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and [head of government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") of the [United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States "United States"),[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTERossiter196286-1)[indirectly elected](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indirectly_elected "Indirectly elected") to a four-year [term](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Term_of_office "Term of office") via the [Electoral College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Electoral_College "United States Electoral College").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShugart2004633%E2%80%93636-2) Under the [U.S. Constitution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U.S._Constitution "U.S. Constitution"), the officeholder leads the [executive branch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") of the [federal government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") and is the [commander-in-chief](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the [United States Armed Forces](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces").[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpstein2005318-3)

The first president, [George Washington](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"), won a unanimous vote of the Electoral College.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatuz2001xxii-4) The incumbent president is [Donald Trump](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), who [assumed office on January 20, 2025](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_inauguration_of_Donald_Trump "Second inauguration of Donald Trump").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHajela2024-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFreile2024-6) Since the office was established in 1789, 45 men have served in 47 presidencies. The discrepancy is due to the nonconsecutive terms of [Grover Cleveland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland"), counted as the 22nd and 24th president, and Trump, counted as the 45th and 47th president.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAP2024-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchallerWilliams2003192-8)

The presidency of [William Henry Harrison](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Henry_Harrison "William Henry Harrison"), who died 31 days after taking office in 1841, was the shortest in American history.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcHughMackowiak2014990%E2%80%93995-9)[Franklin D. Roosevelt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") served the longest, over twelve years, before dying early in his fourth term in 1945. He is the only U.S. president to have served more than two terms.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkau1974246%E2%80%93275-10) Since the ratification of the [Twenty-second Amendment to the United States Constitution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twenty-second_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-second Amendment to the United States Constitution") in 1951, no person may be elected president more than twice, and no one who has served more than two years of a term to which someone else was elected may be elected more than once.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeabodyGant1999565-11)

Four presidents [died in office](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Death_in_office "Death in office") of natural causes (William Henry Harrison, [Zachary Taylor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zachary_Taylor "Zachary Taylor"), [Warren G. Harding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_G._Harding "Warren G. Harding"), and Franklin D. Roosevelt), four were [assassinated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_United_States_presidential_assassination_attempts_and_plots "List of United States presidential assassination attempts and plots") ([Abraham Lincoln](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), [James A. Garfield](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/James_A._Garfield "James A. Garfield"), [William McKinley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley"), and [John F. Kennedy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy")), and one resigned ([Richard Nixon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), facing [impeachment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impeachment_process_against_Richard_Nixon "Impeachment process against Richard Nixon") and removal from office).[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbott2005627%E2%80%93644-12)[John Tyler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Tyler "John Tyler") was the first [vice president](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States") to assume the presidency during a presidential term, setting the precedent that a vice president who does so becomes the fully functioning president with a new, distinct administration.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDinnerstein1962447%E2%80%93451-13)

Throughout most of its history, [American politics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_politics "American politics") has been dominated by [political parties](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Political_parties_in_the_United_States "Political parties in the United States"). The Constitution is silent on the issue of political parties, and at the time it came into force in 1789, no organized parties existed. Soon after the [1st Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1st_United_States_Congress "1st United States Congress") convened, [political factions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Political_faction "Political faction") began rallying around dominant [Washington administration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Presidency_of_George_Washington "Presidency of George Washington") officials, such as [Alexander Hamilton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton") and [Thomas Jefferson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson").[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Guide_to_U.S._Elections''2010197Nardulli1992179-14) Concerned about the capacity of political parties to destroy the fragile unity holding the nation together, Washington remained [unaffiliated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") with any political faction or party throughout his eight-year presidency. He was, and remains, the only U.S. president who never affiliated with a political party.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTELOC_(2)Jamison2014-15)

Notes
-----

1.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-16)**Presidents are numbered according to uninterrupted periods served by the same person. For example, George Washington served two consecutive terms and is counted as the first president, instead of the first and second, but Grover Cleveland and Donald Trump are counted twice because their two terms were not consecutive. A vice president who temporarily becomes [acting president](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acting_President_of_the_United_States "Acting President of the United States") under the [Twenty-fifth Amendment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution") to the Constitution is not counted, because the president remains in office during such a period.
2.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-18)**Reflects the president's political party at the start of their presidency. Changes during their time in office are noted. Also reflects the vice president's political party unless otherwise noted beside the individual's name.
3.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-23)**Political parties had not been anticipated when the Constitution was drafted, nor did they exist at the time of the first presidential election in 1788–89. When they did develop, during Washington's first term, Adams joined the faction that became the [Federalist Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federalist_Party "Federalist Party"). The elections of 1792 were the first ones in the United States that were contested on anything resembling a partisan basis.[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Guide_to_U.S._Elections''2010197,_272Nardulli1992179-22)
4.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-26)**The 1796 presidential election was the first contested American presidential election and the only one in which a president and vice president were elected from opposing political parties. Federalist John Adams was elected president, and Jefferson of the Democratic-Republicans was elected vice president.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Guide_to_U.S._Elections''2010274-25)
5.   ^ [_**a**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-0)[_**b**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-1)[_**c**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-2)[_**d**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-3)[_**e**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-4)[_**f**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-5)[_**g**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-6)[_**h**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-7)[_**i**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-8)[_**j**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-9)[_**k**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-10)[_**l**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-11)[_**m**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-12)[_**n**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-13)[_**o**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-diedintraterm_30-14)Died in office[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeale200422-29)
6.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-JQAdams_34-0)**Early during John Quincy Adams' term, the [Democratic-Republican Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party") dissolved; his allies in Congress and at the state level were referred to as "Adams' Men" during the Adams presidency. When [Andrew Jackson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") became president in 1829, this group became the "Anti-Jackson" [opposition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opposition_(politics) "Opposition (politics)"), and organized themselves as the [National Republican Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Republican_Party "National Republican Party").[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Guide_to_U.S._Elections''2010228Goldman1951159-33)
7.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-36)**John Calhoun, formerly a Democratic-Republican, founded the [Nullifier Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nullifier_Party "Nullifier Party") in 1828 to oppose the [Tariff of 1828](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tariff_of_Abominations "Tariff of Abominations") and advance the cause of [states' rights](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/States%27_rights "States' rights"), but was brought on as Andrew Jackson's running mate in the 1828 presidential election in an effort to broaden the democratic coalition led by Jackson.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Guide_to_U.S._Elections''2010892Houpt201026,_280-35)
8.   ^ [_**a**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-resignedintraterm_38-0)[_**b**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-resignedintraterm_38-1)[_**c**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-resignedintraterm_38-2)Resigned from office[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeale200422-29)
9.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-43)**John Tyler succeeded to the presidency upon the death of William Henry Harrison.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbott201323-42)
10.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-JTyler_45-0)**John Tyler was elected vice president on the Whig Party ticket in 1840. His policy priorities as president soon proved to be opposed to most of the Whig agenda, and he was expelled from the party five months after assuming office.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTECash201834%E2%80%9336-44)
11.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-50)**Millard Fillmore succeeded to the presidency upon the death of Zachary Taylor.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbott2005639-49)
12.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-ALincoln_55-0)**When he ran for reelection in 1864, Republican Abraham Lincoln formed a bipartisan [electoral alliance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electoral_alliance "Electoral alliance") with [War Democrats](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/War_Democrat "War Democrat") by selecting Democrat Andrew Johnson as his running mate, and running on the [National Union Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Union_Party_(United_States) "National Union Party (United States)") ticket.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcSeveney1986139-54)
13.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-57)**Andrew Johnson succeeded to the presidency upon the death of Abraham Lincoln.[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrefousse2000-56)
14.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-AJohnson_58-0)**While president, Andrew Johnson tried and failed to build a coalition of loyalists under the National Union banner. Near the end of his presidency, Johnson began reassociating with the Democratic Party.[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrefousse2000-56)
15.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-64)**Chester A. Arthur succeeded to the presidency upon the death of James A. Garfield.[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreenberger2017174%E2%80%93175-63)
16.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-70)**Theodore Roosevelt succeeded to the presidency upon the death of William McKinley.[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbott2005639%E2%80%93640-69)
17.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-76)**Calvin Coolidge succeeded to the presidency upon the death of Warren G. Harding.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTESenate-75)
18.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-81)**Harry S. Truman succeeded to the presidency upon the death of Franklin D. Roosevelt.[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbott2005636-80)
19.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-86)**Lyndon B. Johnson succeeded to the presidency upon the death of John F. Kennedy.[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbott2005633-85)
20.   ^ [_**a**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-VP25th_88-0)[_**b**_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-VP25th_88-1)Appointed as vice president under terms of the [Twenty-fifth Amendment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), Section 2[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeale200422-29)
21.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_ref-90)**Gerald Ford succeeded to the presidency upon the resignation of Richard Nixon. Even though Ford served out the remainder of Nixon's second term, he was never elected to the presidency or vice presidency.[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreene2013-89)

Donald J. Trump
===============

45th & 47th President of the United States

![Image 2: President Donald J. Trump Official Presidential Portrait](https://www.whitehouse.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/President-Donald-Trump-Official-Presidential-Portrait.png)

After a landslide election victory in 2024, President Donald J. Trump is returning to the White House to build upon his previous successes and use his mandate to reject the extremist policies of the radical left while providing tangible quality of life improvements for the American people.

This includes putting a stop to endless wars, defending our nation’s borders, and unleashing the potential of the American economy, affording ALL Americans the opportunity to pursue THEIR version of the American dream.

In his first administration, President Trump passed record-setting tax cuts and regulation cuts, achieved energy independence, replaced NAFTA with the United-States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, invested $2 trillion to completely rebuild the Military, launched the Space Force, obliterated the ISIS Caliphate, achieved a major breakthrough for peace in the Middle East, passed the most significant Veterans Affairs reforms in half a century, confirmed over 250 federal judges, including 3 Supreme Court Justices, signed bipartisan Criminal Justice Reform, lowered drug prices, protected Medicare and Social Security, and secured our nation’s borders in his first four years in office. This was only the beginning.

Donald J. Trump defines the American success story. Throughout his life he has continually set the standards of business and entrepreneurial excellence, especially in real estate, sports, and entertainment. President Trump built on his success in private life when he entered into politics and public service.

He remarkably won the Presidency in his first ever run for any political office. He won a second time despite several assassination attempts and the unprecedented weaponization of law fare against him. Time after time President Trump has defied the odds because he fundamentally believes in the exceptionalism of the American spirit and wants to see our great country succeed.

A graduate of the University of Pennsylvania’s Wharton School of Finance, President Trump followed in his father’s footsteps into the world of real estate development, making his mark in New York City. There, the Trump name soon became synonymous with the most prestigious of addresses in Manhattan and, subsequently, throughout the world.

President Trump is also an accomplished author. He has written more than fourteen bestsellers. His first book, The Art of the Deal, is considered a business classic.

President Trump has five children, Barron, Don Jr., Ivanka, Eric, and Tiffany, as well as 11 grandchildren.

[Learn more about First Lady Melania Trump here.](https://www.whitehouse.gov/the-administration/melania-trump/)
